text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic cholecystectomy using four standard ports. The gallbladder is grasped at the fundus and retracted upwards. The gallbladder is held at Hartman's pouch while the posterior peritoneum is dissected with a hook diathermy. Careful dissection is conducted in the calyx region.

The surgeon uses hook diathermy to divide both posterior and anterior peritoneal attachments of the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring the dissection remains above the rubeus sulcus to prevent injury to the common bile duct and other vital structures. The cystic duct is dissected free, and further separation of the gallbladder from the liver bed is visually observed.

The surgeon applies two clips below and one clip above the cystic duct, then divides it using cold scissors. The cystic artery is skeletonized, dissected, clipped, and divided using hook diathermy due to its thin structure.
The surgeon uses hook diathermy to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed, taking care to avoid creating a hole in the gallbladder that could lead to bile and stone spillage.
The surgeon completes the removal of the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring hemostasis as needed. The gallbladder specimen is then retrieved through the epigastric port.
